answer:
**Charles Comte's foundational position on liberty** appears primarily in *Traité de la propriété*, Vol. I, Chapters 2–3 (early in the frontmatter after the preface). There, he frames liberty as the essential precondition for exercising *all* rights and fulfilling *all* duties inherent in human nature.[^1] He defines it negatively and relationally: "the state of a person who encounters, in his fellow men, no obstacle, whether to the regular development of his being, or to the innocent exercise of his faculties."[^2] Liberty is the antithesis of slavery (civil or political), where one person treats another as property—seizing labor products, directing work, restricting movement, association, education, arms, etc. Without liberty, duties (to self, family, humanity) and rights collapse into contradiction; one cannot legitimately abdicate liberty even voluntarily.[^3] It demands collective protection via society's combined forces against violence or extortion, while each directs their own labor and rests per needs/interests.[^4]

**Charles Dunoyer's foundational position** is laid out in *Nouveau traité d'économie*, Vol. I, Introduction and Chapter 1 (the front). He defines liberty positively as "*power* [...] to use his forces more easily *as he frees himself* from the obstacles that originally hindered their exercise"—a relative quantity enlarging with civilization.[^5] Obstacles are *internal* (ignorance/inertia, passions harming self/others) or *external* (violence/injustice from others), overcome via three falsifiable conditions: (1) *develop faculties* (industry/skill/knowledge); (2) *self-regulate* to avoid self-harm (prudence/moderation); (3) *refrain from harming others* (justice/non-aggression, with resistance to secure it).[^6] Liberty grows with moral/industrial progress across societal stages (savage → nomadic → slaveholding → serfdom → privilege → pure industry); no innate "right" exists without these capacities—declamations alone fail.[^7]

**Dunoyer's definition is broader**: Comte's is narrower/external, zeroing in on *interpersonal non-domination* (no human obstacles to innocent action/development, enabling rights/duties)—a binary threshold against slavery's mechanisms. Dunoyer's spans *personal/internal* barriers too (faculties undeveloped, unregulated), tying liberty to graduated *self-mastery* via empirical progress, while including Comte-like non-harm but subordinating it to broader civilization.

**Dunoyer's attaches more falsifiable conditions**: Comte's are observable absences (e.g., no forced labor direction, no passes/movement bans, free association/arms/education/child-rearing), testable via slavery's presence but fewer gradients/predictions.[^8] Dunoyer's multiply-testable via progress metrics: predict more liberty where faculties develop (e.g., civilized > savage in skill/security), self-control grows (temperate > debauched), non-aggression/resistance prevails (industrious USA > despotic Turkey); empirically regressible if vices/ignorance return.[^9]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, ch. 3, [translation](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/#p-9b6829).

[^2]: Ibid., [para. p-1c5676](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/#p-1c5676).

[^3]: Ibid., ch. 2, [para. p-3114c0](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/02-de-la-liberte-consideree-comme-une-condition-de-le/translation/#p-3114c0); ch. 3, [para. p-2cd7a3](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/#p-2cd7a3).

[^4]: Ibid., ch. 3, [paras. p-86c544–p-a8db3b](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/#p-86c544).

[^5]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, ch. 1, [para. p-fc8999](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-fc8999).

[^6]: Ibid., [paras. p-c996f5–p-1abed5](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-c996f5); Introduction, [para. p-67acca](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/00-introduction/translation/#p-67acca).

[^7]: Ibid., ch. 1, [paras. p-583c6c–p-e80d8f](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-583c6c); Introduction, [paras. p-208fe2–p-de302e](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/00-introduction/translation/#p-208fe2).

[^8]: Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, ch. 3, [paras. p-b1dcd2–p-7964db](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/#p-b1dcd2).

[^9]: Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie*, ch. 1, [paras. p-51f10e–p-709973](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-51f10e).
